text: The first stage of the procedure is the left lateral sectionectomy.
This firstly involves a laparoscopy to identify the liver metastasis and to rule out extrahepatic spread.
The left liver is then mobilised by dividing the left triangular ligament with a hooked diaphragm.
A swab is placed under the liver to protect both the oesophagus and the stomach.
A nylon tape can also be placed around the left lobe of the liver and can be used to provide traction during the transection of the liver parenchyma.
The division of the left coronary ligament to the level of the left hepatic vein completes the mobilisation of the left liver.
Intraoperative ultrasound scanning is then used to confirm the position of the metastatic disease and to rule out other lesions that are not seen on preoperative scanning.
The transection line is then marked out with a hooked diaphragm on the surface of the liver.
For a left lateral sectionectomy, this is at the level of a falciform ligament.
Transection of the liver parenchyma is achieved using the Lotus liver resector.
The Lotus system is a torsional ultrasonic scalpel which delivers energy perpendicular to its blade.
The transection begins at the ligamentum teres and continues up the premarked line which is parallel to the falciform ligament.
The deeper transection then continues just lateral to the falciform and round ligament complex.
The first portal structure to be encountered is the left portal inflow.
Once identified, this can be safely divided using a laparoscopic endo-GIA stapler.
The transection then continues using the LOTUS energy device up into segment 2 of the liver where the left outflow structures will be encountered.
Once the left hepatic vein has been identified, this can again be safely divided by the laparoscopic endo-GIA vascular stapler.
More than one stapling of the EndoGI stapler can be used to ensure that the left hepatic vein is sealed and ligated.
The Lotus Energy device can safely seal vessels or pedicles less than five millimeters in diameter.
The Pringle manoeuvre is not usually required for a left lateral sectionectomy.
The transection is completed at the level of the left triangular ligament as shown here where it can be safely dissected using the lotus.
The transection plane is then checked for haemostasis as the central venous pressure is increased by the anaesthetist following the transection.
Haemostatic adjuncts such as floceal and tisseal can both also be used.
Any bile leakage should also be dealt with at this stage.
The right hemicolectomy is usually performed as the second stage of this procedure.
The cecum in this case is particularly inflamed and adhered to both the omentum and the anterior abdominal wall.
The adhesions are safely divided using a Ligishore energy device with a Maryland tip.
The cecum is separated here from the anterior abdominal wall.
The mobilization of the right colon begins here and in this case using a medial to lateral approach.
The peritoneum is divided on the medial side of the mesocolon and is pushed down to achieve a total mesocolic excision for best oncological outcome.
Dissection is performed down to the origin of the ileocolic artery.
This is also the case for the middle colic and the right colic arteries.
At this stage, both the ureter and the gonadal vessels are identified and preserved.
In this patient's case, an emmental resection was performed for oncological reasons as the emmentum was firmly adhered to the cecum and the anterior abdominal wall.
Here we show a laparoscopic dance where one Johan
is moved during one cycle of a ligature ligation in an alternating fashion to divide the omentum.
The omentum is divided up to the level of a transverse colon and the transverse colon is then moved cordially.
The lesser sac is then exposed and entered
Following this, the lateral dissection of the right colon is completed by dividing the lateral peritoneal folds.
This is again achieved by the ligature.
Following this, the right colon will be fully mobilised and can be moved medially in preparation for the colonic resection and subsequent anastomosis.
Here, the duodenum and the head of the pancreas can both be identified, as well as the ileocolic pedicle, which has already been ligated and divided and secured with haemlocks.
The ileocolic artery, the right colic and the middle colic arteries have all been divided at their origins of the SMA.
An endocatch device is then inserted through one of the working 12mm ports and the liver specimen is placed within the device, ready for retrieval.
Finally, the mobilized cecum is secured with a Johan.
